title: Vignette
text: tidyMacro R package fast estimation Vector Autoregression (VAR) models via C++ (Rcpp/RcppArmadillo). : (updated extend functionality package). Fast VAR & VARX reduced form estimations Zero dependency packages. Ground written C++. visualizations made ggplot2 R. Identification via external instruments (popular) classical recursive methods Impulse Response Functions bootstrap confidence bands (moving blocks, residual, wild) Bias-corrected bootstraps (Pope correction) Forecast Error Variance Decomposition (FEVD) Historical Decomposition Publication-ready plots box. plot #ggplot2 object, can ex post customized Parallel bootstrap computations via #OpenMP maximum speed Excellent documentation detailed examples every function

title: Vignette
code: # Install from GitHub devtools::install_github("muhsinciftci/tidyMacro")

title: Vignette
code: library(tidyMacro)

what: Replication 1: @känzig2021 — Oil Supply News Shocks via External Instruments
title: Vignette
text: package calculates many intermediate steps. However, One can manually calculate IV steps original paper:
code: library(tidyMacro) library(tidyverse) #> ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ── #> ✔ dplyr     1.2.0     ✔ readr     2.1.5 #> ✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.6.0 #> ✔ ggplot2   4.0.2     ✔ tibble    3.3.1 #> ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2 #> ✔ purrr     1.2.1      #> ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ── #> ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter() #> ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag() #> ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors library(tictoc) library(patchwork)  set_tidyMacro_theme() #> tidyMacro theme and color scales set as default for this session.  # Load data data("kaenzig_data") kaenzig_data |> head() #> # A tibble: 6 × 8 #>   date                Oil_Price World_Oil_Prod World_Oil_Inven World_IP US_IP #>   <dttm>                  <dbl>          <dbl>           <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 1974-01-01 00:00:00      307.          1092.            628.     379.  385. #> 2 1974-02-01 00:00:00      306.          1093.            632.     378.  385. #> 3 1974-03-01 00:00:00      305.          1094.            631.     378.  385. #> 4 1974-04-01 00:00:00      305.          1095.            634.     378.  385. #> 5 1974-05-01 00:00:00      304.          1096.            638.     379.  385. #> 6 1974-06-01 00:00:00      303.          1095.            640.     378.  385. #> # ℹ 2 more variables: US_CPI <dbl>, iv_kanzig_final <dbl> # Pass data as matrix finaldata <- kaenzig_data |>   select(Oil_Price, World_Oil_Prod, World_Oil_Inven, World_IP, US_IP, US_CPI) |>   as.matrix()  iv_oil <- kaenzig_data |>   select(iv_kanzig_final) |>   drop_na() |>   as.matrix()  varnames <- c(   "Oil Price",   "World Oil Prod.",   "World Oil Inven.",   "World IP",   "US IP",   "US CPI" )  shockname <- "Oil Shock"  # Estimate VAR T <- nrow(finaldata) N <- ncol(finaldata) c <- 1 p <- 12  var_result <- fVAR(finaldata, p, c) residuals <- var_result$residuals sigma_full <- var_result$sigma_full  # Wold IRFs hor <- 48 wold <- fwoldIRF(fVAR = var_result, horizon = hor)  # I use 100 bootstraps here for replications but you should use 1000 or so nboots <- 100  # Moving block bootstrap result_mbb <- fbootstrapIV_mbb(   y = finaldata,   var_result = var_result,   Z = iv_oil,   nboot = nboots,   blocksize = 0, # 0 means Automatic selection   adjustZ = c(1, 417),   adjustu = c(100, 516),   policyvar = 1,   horizon = hor,   prc = 90 ) #> Using 3 thread(s) for parallel bootstrap computation...  # Plot IRFs fplotirf_iv(   point = result_mbb$meanirf * 10,   upper = result_mbb$upper * 10,   lower = result_mbb$lower * 10,   varnames = varnames,   shockname = shockname,   prc = 90,   facet_ncol = 3 ) +   labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) # IV identification u_p <- residuals[, 1] u_p_final <- u_p[(length(u_p) - length(iv_oil) + 1):length(u_p)] |> as.matrix() u_q_final <- residuals[   (nrow(residuals) - length(iv_oil) + 1):nrow(residuals),   2:ncol(residuals) ] |>   as.matrix()  u <- residuals[(nrow(residuals) - length(iv_oil) + 1):nrow(residuals), ] T1 <- nrow(u) sigma <- (t(u) %*% u) / (T1 - 1 - p - N * p) S <- t(chol(sigma))  ols_result1 <- fOLS(y = u_p_final, X = iv_oil, 0) uhat <- ols_result1$fitted_partial sq_sp <- solve(t(uhat) %*% uhat) %*% t(uhat) %*% u_q_final  s <- c(1, sq_sp[1], sq_sp[2], sq_sp[3], sq_sp[4], sq_sp[5])  # Structural IRFs ivirf <- matrix(0, nrow = N, ncol = hor + 1) for (h in 1:(hor + 1)) {   ivirf[, h] <- wold[,, h] %*% s }

previous_headings: tidyMacro > Replication 1: @känzig2021 — Oil Supply News Shocks via External Instruments
title: Vignette
code: # FEVD fevd_result <- fevd_iv(s, S, wold, N, hor, sigma, u, T1 = T1, p = p)  fplot_vardec(   fevd = fevd_result$fevd_iv,   varnames = varnames,   shocknames = shockname ) # FEVD scaled to unit variance (Känzig approach) scaler <- s / norm(solve(S) %*% s, type = "2") ivirf_scaled <- matrix(0, nrow = N, ncol = hor + 1) for (h in 1:(hor + 1)) {   ivirf_scaled[, h] <- wold[,, h] %*% scaler }  temp <- matrix(0, nrow = N, ncol = N) denom <- matrix(0, nrow = N, ncol = hor + 1) for (h in 1:(hor + 1)) {   temp <- temp + wold[,, h] %*% sigma %*% t(wold[,, h])   denom[, h] <- diag(temp) }  irf_sq <- ivirf_scaled^2 num <- t(apply(irf_sq, 1, cumsum)) fevd_iv <- num / denom  fplot_vardec(   fevd = fevd_iv,   varnames = varnames,   shocknames = shockname ) +   scale_fill_manual(values = tidyMacro_colors[c(2, 3)])

what: Replication 2: Bloom (2009) — Uncertainty Shocks via Cholesky Ordering
title: Vignette
code: library(tidyMacro) library(tidyverse)  # Load data data("bloom2009") df0 <- bloom2009  dates_vec <- df0 |> pull(Date) y <- df0 |> select(-Date) |> as.matrix()  # Estimate VAR p <- 12 c <- 1 var_bloom <- fVAR(y, p, c)  sigma <- var_bloom$sigma_full S <- t(chol(sigma))  horizon <- 48 wold <- fwoldIRF(fVAR = var_bloom, horizon = horizon) point_irf <- fcholeskyIRF(wold, S)  # Bootstrap confidence bands prc <- 68  bloom_chol <- fbootstrapChol(   y = y,   var_result = var_bloom,   nboot = nboots,   horizon = 48,   prc = 90,   bootscheme = "residual" )

previous_headings: tidyMacro > Replication 2: Bloom (2009) — Uncertainty Shocks via Cholesky Ordering
title: Vignette
code: # Plot IRF shockname <- "UNCERT" var_names <- colnames(y) shock <- match(shockname, var_names)  fplotirf_chol(   point_irf,   bloom_chol$upper,   bloom_chol$lower,   shock = shock,   varnames = var_names,   prc = 68,   facet_ncol = 4 ) +   ftheme_tidyMacro()

previous_headings: tidyMacro > Replication 2: Bloom (2009) — Uncertainty Shocks via Cholesky Ordering
title: Vignette
code: # FEVD vardec <- fevd_chol(point_irf, shock = shock)  fplot_vardec(   fevd = vardec$fevd,   varnames = var_names,   shocknames = var_names[shock] ) +   ftheme_tidyMacro() +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("gray95", "lightblue"))

previous_headings: tidyMacro > Replication 2: Bloom (2009) — Uncertainty Shocks via Cholesky Ordering
title: Vignette
code: series <- setdiff(seq_len(ncol(y)), shock)  histdec_list <- setNames(   lapply(series, function(i) fhistdec(y, var_bloom, S, i)$histdec),   colnames(y)[series] )  fplot_histdec(   histdec_list = histdec_list,   shock = shock,   shockname = shockname,   dates = dates_vec,   p = p,   facet_ncol = 2,   return_data = FALSE ) +   scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "7 years", date_labels = "%Y") +   ftheme_tidyMacro()

previous_headings: tidyMacro > Replication 2: Bloom (2009) — Uncertainty Shocks via Cholesky Ordering
title: Vignette
code: # Bias-corrected Bootstrap nboot1 <- 1000 nboot2 <- 2000  tic() result_corrected <- fbootstrapCholCorrected(   y = y,   var_result = var_bloom,   nboot1 = nboots,   nboot2 = nboots,   horizon = 48,   prc = prc,   bootscheme = "residual" ) toc() #> 3.995 sec elapsed  fplotirf_chol(   point_irf,   result_corrected$upper,   result_corrected$lower,   shock = shock,   varnames = var_names,   prc = prc,   facet_ncol = 4 ) +   ftheme_tidyMacro()

what: Determine Optimal Lag Order for VAR Model Using Information Criteria — fAICBIC
title: Determine Optimal Lag Order for VAR Model Using Information Criteria — fAICBIC
text: Fits VAR models lag orders 1 pmax returns lag length minimises three information criteria.

title: Determine Optimal Lag Order for VAR Model Using Information Criteria — fAICBIC
code: fAICBIC(y, pmax, c, exog = NULL)

title: Determine Optimal Lag Order for VAR Model Using Information Criteria — fAICBIC
text: y T x N matrix endogenous variables (T observations, N series). pmax integer specifying maximum VAR lag order consider. c integer (0 1) indicating whether include constant term (1 = include, 0 = exclude). exog optional T x M matrix exogenous variables. Default NULL.

title: Determine Optimal Lag Order for VAR Model Using Information Criteria — fAICBIC
text: list three integer elements: aic — optimal lag length   Akaike IC; bic — optimal lag length Bayesian IC; hq — optimal lag   length Hannan-Quinn IC.

title: Determine Optimal Lag Order for VAR Model Using Information Criteria — fAICBIC
text: candidate lag order p residual covariance Sigma = (e'e)/T computed three criteria evaluated : AIC = log(det(Sigma)) + 2 * (p*N^2 + N + M) / T BIC = log(det(Sigma)) + log(T) * (p*N^2 + N + M) / T HQ  = log(det(Sigma)) + 2 * log(log(T)) * (p*N^2 + N + M) / T M number exogenous variables.

title: Determine Optimal Lag Order for VAR Model Using Information Criteria — fAICBIC
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ set.seed(123) y      <- matrix(rnorm(200), ncol = 2) result <- fAICBIC(y, pmax = 10, c = 1) result$aic result$bic } # }

title: Ordinary Least Squares Regression — fOLS
text: Ordinary Least Squares Regression

title: Ordinary Least Squares Regression — fOLS
code: fOLS(y, X, c = 1L)

title: Ordinary Least Squares Regression — fOLS
text: y Dependent variable matrix! vector X Independent variables matrix (T x N) c Integer indicator intercept (1 intercept included, 0 otherwise)

title: Ordinary Least Squares Regression — fOLS
text: list containing: beta: Coefficient estimates ((N+1) x 1 c=1, N x 1 c=0) fitted: Fitted values (T x 1) err: Residuals (T x 1) r2: R-squared statistic (scalar) fitted_partial: Fitted values excluding intercept (T x 1)

title: Ordinary Least Squares Regression — fOLS
text: function performs ordinary least squares (OLS) regression. coefficient estimates computed using normal equations: $$\hat{\beta} = (X'X)^{-1}X'y$$ R-squared statistic measures proportion variance explained: $$R^2 = 1 - \frac{RSS}{TSS}$$ RSS residual sum squares TSS total sum squares. intercept included (c=1), fitted_partial contains fitted values excluding intercept contribution, useful assessing explanatory power regressors alone.

title: Ordinary Least Squares Regression — fOLS
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Generate sample data set.seed(123) y <- rnorm(100) X <- matrix(rnorm(200), 100, 2)  # OLS with intercept result <- fOLS(y, X, c = 1) print(result$beta) print(result$r2)  # OLS without intercept result_no_int <- fOLS(y, X, c = 0) } # }

title: Print SVAR Identification Steps — fSVAR_steps
text: Prints numbered guide proxy-SVAR identification invertibility-testing procedure R console.

title: Print SVAR Identification Steps — fSVAR_steps
code: fSVAR_steps()

title: Print SVAR Identification Steps — fSVAR_steps
text: Called side-effect printing console.   Returns NULL invisibly.

title: Print SVAR Identification Steps — fSVAR_steps
text: Step 1: Estimate VAR(p) extract residuals. Step 2: Regress instrument current residuals plus f leads; save fitted value. Step 3: F-test whether lead coefficients jointly zero. Rejection implies shock non-invertible. Step 4: invertible, apply standard Stock-Watson IV procedure obtain IRFs unit-variance shocks. Step 5: non-invertible, test recoverability via Ljung-Box test fitted instrument series. Step 6: recoverable (null autocorrelation rejected), repeat IV procedure adding residual leads first-stage regression. Step 7: recoverability rejected, SVAR analysis instrument feasible.

title: Print SVAR Identification Steps — fSVAR_steps
code: fSVAR_steps() #> Step 1: Estimate the VAR(p) and extract the residuals. #> Step 2: Regress the instrument on current residuals plus f leads. Save the fitted value. #> Step 3: F-test that lead coefficients are jointly zero. Rejection implies non-invertibility. #> Step 4: If invertible, apply the Stock-Watson IV procedure for IRFs and unit-variance shocks. #> Step 5: If non-invertible, test recoverability via the Ljung-Box test on the fitted instrument. #> Step 6: If recoverable, redo the IV procedure adding residual leads to the first-stage regression. #> Step 7: If recoverability is rejected, SVAR analysis with this instrument is not feasible.

title: Vector Autoregression (VAR) Model Estimation — fVAR
text: Estimates Vector Autoregression model optional exogenous variables using ordinary least squares (OLS). Optimised maximum speed memory efficiency via RcppArmadillo.

title: Vector Autoregression (VAR) Model Estimation — fVAR
code: fVAR(y, p, c, exog = NULL)

title: Vector Autoregression (VAR) Model Estimation — fVAR
text: y numeric matrix time series data T rows (time periods) N columns (endogenous variables). p integer specifying number lags include VAR. c integer indicator including constant term (1 = include intercept, 0 = intercept). exog optional T x M matrix exogenous variables. provided, variables enter contemporaneously (lagged). Default NULL.

title: Vector Autoregression (VAR) Model Estimation — fVAR
text: list elements: beta — coefficient matrix dimensions   (Np + c + M) x N, first row intercept (c = 1),   followed N*p lag-coefficient rows, M exogenous-coefficient rows;   residuals — (T-p) x N matrix OLS residuals;   sigma_full — N x N residual covariance matrix normalised (n_obs - 1);   p — lag order (echoed input);   c — intercept indicator (echoed input);   n_exog — number exogenous variables (0 none provided).

title: Vector Autoregression (VAR) Model Estimation — fVAR
text: model $$Y_t = c + A_1 Y_{t-1} + \cdots + A_p Y_{t-p} + B X_t + e_t$$ \(X_t\) contemporaneous exogenous variables. Without exogenous variables model reduces standard VAR(p). Estimation uses arma::solve numerical stability.

title: Vector Autoregression (VAR) Model Estimation — fVAR
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # VAR(2) without exogenous variables y      <- matrix(rnorm(200), ncol = 2) result <- fVAR(y, p = 2, c = 1)  # VAR(2) with one exogenous variable exog   <- matrix(rnorm(100), ncol = 1) result <- fVAR(y, p = 2, c = 1, exog = exog) } # }

what: Vector Autoregression with Exogenous Variables (VARX) Model Estimation — fVARX
title: Vector Autoregression with Exogenous Variables (VARX) Model Estimation — fVARX
text: Estimates VARX(p) model endogenous exogenous variables lagged using ordinary least squares (OLS). Optimised maximum speed memory efficiency.

title: Vector Autoregression with Exogenous Variables (VARX) Model Estimation — fVARX
code: fVARX(y, ex, p, c)

title: Vector Autoregression with Exogenous Variables (VARX) Model Estimation — fVARX
text: y numeric matrix endogenous time series data T rows (time periods) N columns (variables). ex numeric matrix exogenous time series data T rows (time periods) M columns (variables). p integer specifying number lags endogenous exogenous variables. c integer indicator including constant term (1 = include intercept, 0 = intercept).

title: Vector Autoregression with Exogenous Variables (VARX) Model Estimation — fVARX
text: list two elements: beta — coefficient matrix dimensions   (Np + M + Mp + c) x N; residuals — (T-p) x N residual matrix.

title: Vector Autoregression with Exogenous Variables (VARX) Model Estimation — fVARX
text: VARX(p) model : $$Y_t = c + A_1 Y_{t-1} + \cdots + A_p Y_{t-p}            + B_0 X_t + B_1 X_{t-1} + \cdots + B_p X_{t-p} + e_t$$ endogenous exogenous variables lagged p periods. Exogenous variables also enter contemporaneously time t.

title: Vector Autoregression with Exogenous Variables (VARX) Model Estimation — fVARX
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ y  <- matrix(rnorm(200), ncol = 2) ex <- matrix(rnorm(100), ncol = 1) result    <- fVARX(y, ex, p = 2, c = 1) beta      <- result$beta residuals <- result$residuals } # }

what: Bootstrap Cholesky Impulse Response Functions — fbootstrapChol
title: Bootstrap Cholesky Impulse Response Functions — fbootstrapChol
text: Bootstrap Cholesky Impulse Response Functions

title: Bootstrap Cholesky Impulse Response Functions — fbootstrapChol
code: fbootstrapChol(   y,   var_result,   nboot,   horizon,   prc,   bootscheme,   exog = NULL,   n_threads = 0L )

title: Bootstrap Cholesky Impulse Response Functions — fbootstrapChol
text: y T x N matrix original endogenous variables. var_result List fVAR. nboot Number bootstrap replications. horizon Maximum IRF horizon. prc Confidence level percent (e.g. 68). bootscheme "residual" "wild". exog Optional T x M exogenous matrix. Default NULL. n_threads OpenMP threads. 0 = cores minus one.

title: Bootstrap Cholesky Impulse Response Functions — fbootstrapChol
text: List: bootchol, upper, lower, boot_beta.

what: Bias-Corrected Bootstrap Cholesky IRF Confidence Bands (Kilian 1998) — fbootstrapCholCorrected
title: Bias-Corrected Bootstrap Cholesky IRF Confidence Bands (Kilian 1998) — fbootstrapCholCorrected
text: Bias-Corrected Bootstrap Cholesky IRF Confidence Bands (Kilian 1998)

title: Bias-Corrected Bootstrap Cholesky IRF Confidence Bands (Kilian 1998) — fbootstrapCholCorrected
code: fbootstrapCholCorrected(   y,   var_result,   nboot1,   nboot2,   horizon,   prc,   bootscheme,   exog = NULL,   n_threads = 0L )

title: Bias-Corrected Bootstrap Cholesky IRF Confidence Bands (Kilian 1998) — fbootstrapCholCorrected
text: y T x N matrix endogenous variables. var_result List returned fVAR(). nboot1 First-pass bootstrap replications bias estimation. nboot2 Second-pass bootstrap replications confidence bands. horizon Maximum IRF horizon. prc Confidence level percent (e.g. 68 90). bootscheme "residual" "wild". exog Optional T x M matrix exogenous variables. Default NULL. n_threads OpenMP threads. 0 = cores minus one.

title: Bias-Corrected Bootstrap Cholesky IRF Confidence Bands (Kilian 1998) — fbootstrapCholCorrected
text: List: bootchol, upper, lower,   boot_beta, Beta, corrections.

title: Bootstrap VAR Model — fbootstrapVAR
text: Generates bootstrap pseudo-sample fitted VAR model using either residual (iid) resampling wild (Rademacher) bootstrap scheme.

title: Bootstrap VAR Model — fbootstrapVAR
code: fbootstrapVAR(y, fVAR_result, bootscheme = "residual")

title: Bootstrap VAR Model — fbootstrapVAR
text: y T x N matrix original endogenous variables. fVAR_result List fVAR containing beta, residuals, p, c. bootscheme String indicating bootstrap method: "residual" iid resampling residuals, "wild" Rademacher wild bootstrap.

title: Bootstrap VAR Model — fbootstrapVAR
text: list two elements: ynext — T x N bootstrapped data matrix   (first p rows copied y); rademacher — T_eff-length vector   Rademacher signs used wild scheme (zeros residual scheme).

title: Bootstrap VAR Model — fbootstrapVAR
text: recursion $$y_t = c + A_1 y_{t-1} + \cdots + A_p y_{t-p} + e_t^*$$ \(e_t^*\) either randomly resampled residual (residual scheme) t-th residual multiplied Rademacher sign (wild scheme).

what: Compute Cholesky Impulse Response Functions — fcholeskyIRF
title: Compute Cholesky Impulse Response Functions — fcholeskyIRF
text: Compute Cholesky Impulse Response Functions

title: Compute Cholesky Impulse Response Functions — fcholeskyIRF
code: fcholeskyIRF(wold, S)

title: Compute Cholesky Impulse Response Functions — fcholeskyIRF
text: wold Wold representation cube (N x N x horizon) S Cholesky factor matrix (N x N), lower triangular

title: Compute Cholesky Impulse Response Functions — fcholeskyIRF
text: cube containing:   Cholesky structural impulse response functions (N x N x horizon)

title: Compute Cholesky Impulse Response Functions — fcholeskyIRF
text: function computes structural impulse response functions using Cholesky decomposition identification scheme. horizon h, structural IRF computed : $$IRF_h = \Psi_h \cdot S$$ \(\Psi_h\) Wold representation horizon h S Cholesky factor (lower triangular) covariance matrix reduced-form residuals. Cholesky identification imposes recursive structure contemporaneous relationships variables, ordering variables determining causal structure.

title: Compute Cholesky Impulse Response Functions — fcholeskyIRF
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Generate sample Wold representation N <- 3 horizon <- 20 wold <- array(rnorm(N * N * horizon), dim = c(N, N, horizon))  # Compute Cholesky factor Sigma <- matrix(c(1, 0.5, 0.3,                   0.5, 1, 0.4,                   0.3, 0.4, 1), 3, 3) S <- t(chol(Sigma))  # Lower triangular  # Compute Cholesky IRF chol_irf <- fcholeskyIRF(wold, S) } # }

title: Compute VAR Companion Matrix — fcompanionMatrix
text: Compute VAR Companion Matrix

title: Compute VAR Companion Matrix — fcompanionMatrix
code: fcompanionMatrix(beta, c, p)

title: Compute VAR Companion Matrix — fcompanionMatrix
text: beta Coefficient matrix. Dimensions: (Np+c+M) x N, N number variables, p lag order, c 1 intercept included (0 otherwise), M number exogenous variables c Integer indicator intercept (1 intercept included, 0 otherwise) p Integer lag order VAR model

title: Compute VAR Companion Matrix — fcompanionMatrix
text: list containing: comp: Companion matrix (Np x Np) N: Number variables VAR system

title: Compute VAR Companion Matrix — fcompanionMatrix
text: function constructs companion form matrix VAR(p) model. companion matrix representation allows VAR(p) written VAR(1) system, useful various computations including impulse response functions forecasting. exogenous variables present, excluded companion matrix dynamic feedback structure VAR system. companion matrix structure: $$   \begin{bmatrix}   A_1 & A_2 & \cdots & A_{p-1} & A_p \\   I_N & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\   0 & I_N & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\   \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\   0 & 0 & \cdots & I_N & 0   \end{bmatrix} $$

title: Compute VAR Companion Matrix — fcompanionMatrix
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # VAR(2) model with 3 variables, no intercept beta <- matrix(rnorm(18), 6, 3)  # 6 rows (2*3), 3 columns result <- fcompanionMatrix(beta, c = 0, p = 2)  # VAR(2) model with 3 variables, with intercept and 2 exogenous variables beta_int <- matrix(rnorm(27), 9, 3)  # 9 rows (2*3+1+2), 3 columns result <- fcompanionMatrix(beta_int, c = 1, p = 2) } # }

what: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for Cholesky-Identified VARs — fevd_chol
title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for Cholesky-Identified VARs — fevd_chol
text: Computes forecast error variance decomposition (FEVD) showing proportion forecast error variance variable attributable structural shock identified via Cholesky decomposition.

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for Cholesky-Identified VARs — fevd_chol
code: fevd_chol(chol_irf, shock = 0L)

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for Cholesky-Identified VARs — fevd_chol
text: chol_irf N x N x (horizon+1) cube Cholesky impulse response functions fcholeskyIRF. slice chol_irf[,,h] contains responses horizon h. horizon automatically inferred cube dimensions. shock Integer (1-indexed). provided, returns N x horizon matrix specific shock (matching MATLAB variance_decomp(irf, shock)). 0 (default), returns full N x N x horizon cube shocks.

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for Cholesky-Identified VARs — fevd_chol
text: list containing: fevd shock = 0: N x N x horizon cube fevd[,j,h]     share forecast error variance variable horizon h explained     shock j. shock > 0: N x horizon matrix specified shock.

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for Cholesky-Identified VARs — fevd_chol
text: forecast error variance decomposition measures proportion h-step ahead forecast error variance variable attributable structural shock j. Cholesky-identified VARs: $$FEVD(,j,h) = \frac{\sum_{s=0}^{h} [\Psi_s P]_{ij}^2}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} \sum_{s=0}^{h} [\Psi_s P]_{ik}^2}$$

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for Cholesky-Identified VARs — fevd_chol
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Estimate VAR var_result <- fVAR(y, p = 2, c = 1) wold <- fwoldIRF(var_result, horizon = 20) S <- t(chol(var_result$sigma_full)) chol_irf <- fcholeskyIRF(wold, S)  # Full decomposition (all shocks) — returns 3D cube vardec <- fevd_chol(chol_irf)  # Single shock decomposition — returns N x horizon matrix shock <- match("UNCERT", colnames(y)) vardec <- fevd_chol(chol_irf, shock = shock) } # }

what: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for IV-Identified Shocks — fevd_iv
title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for IV-Identified Shocks — fevd_iv
text: Computes forecast error variance decomposition (FEVD) instrumental variable identified structural shocks. function scales shocks unit variance calculates contribution specific shock forecast error variance variable time.

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for IV-Identified Shocks — fevd_iv
code: fevd_iv(s, S, wold, N, hor, sigma, u, T1, p)

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for IV-Identified Shocks — fevd_iv
text: s N x 1 vector structural impact coefficients S N x N lower triangular Cholesky factor residual covariance matrix wold N x N x (hor+1) cube Wold impulse response functions N Integer number variables VAR system hor Integer maximum forecast horizon sigma N x N residual covariance matrix u (T-p) x N matrix VAR residuals T1 Integer effective sample size (T-p) p Integer lag order VAR model

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for IV-Identified Shocks — fevd_iv
text: list containing:   - scaler: N x 1 vector scaling factors unit variance normalization   - ivirf_scaled: N x (hor+1) matrix unit variance impulse responses   - oil_news_unit_var: 1 x T1 vector unit variance shock series   - check_uv: Scalar variance check (close 1)   - denom: N x (hor+1) matrix total forecast error variances   - fevd_iv: N x (hor+1) matrix FEVD shares (0 1)

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for IV-Identified Shocks — fevd_iv
text: FEVD measures proportion h-step ahead forecast error variance variable attributable identified structural shock. function performs following steps: 1. Normalizes structural shock unit variance 2. Computes unit variance impulse responses 3. Recovers unit variance shock series 4. Computes total forecast error variance horizon 5. Computes variance due identified shock 6. Calculates FEVD ratio FEVD values proportions 0 1, higher values indicate identified shock explains larger share forecast error variance.

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for IV-Identified Shocks — fevd_iv
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Estimate VAR and compute Wold IRF var_result <- fVAR(y, p = 2, c = 1) wold <- fwoldIRF(var_result, horizon = 20)  # IV identification s <- c(1, 0.5, 0.3) sigma <- var_result$sigma_full S <- t(chol(sigma))  # Compute FEVD result <- fevd_iv(s, S, wold, N = 3, hor = 20,                   sigma = sigma, u = var_result$residuals,                   T1 = nrow(var_result$residuals), p = 2)  # Plot FEVD for first variable plot(result$fevd_iv[1, ], type = "l", ylim = c(0, 1),      main = "FEVD: Share of Variance Explained by Shock",      xlab = "Horizon", ylab = "Share") } # }

title: Generate VAR Data — fgenerateVARdata
text: Recursively generates VAR data initial values, coefficients, residuals. Optimised maximum performance memory efficiency.

title: Generate VAR Data — fgenerateVARdata
code: fgenerateVARdata(y, p, c, beta, residuals)

title: Generate VAR Data — fgenerateVARdata
text: y T x N matrix initial/historical endogenous variables. p Integer lag order VAR model. c Integer indicator intercept (1 included, 0 otherwise). beta Coefficient matrix: (Np+c) x N c = 1, Np x N c = 0. residuals (T-p) x N matrix residuals/shocks add.

title: Generate VAR Data — fgenerateVARdata
text: T x N matrix generated VAR data. first p rows copied   y; remaining T-p rows generated recursively.

title: Generate VAR Data — fgenerateVARdata
text: recursion follows: $$y_t = c + A_1 y_{t-1} + \cdots + A_p y_{t-p} + e_t$$

title: Generate VAR Data — fgenerateVARdata
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ y_init    <- matrix(rnorm(200), ncol = 2) beta      <- matrix(rnorm(10), 5, 2) residuals <- matrix(rnorm(190), 95, 2) y_new     <- fgenerateVARdata(y_init, p = 2, c = 1, beta, residuals) } # }

what: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
title: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
text: Decomposes chosen variable's realisation contributions structural shock, identified via lower-triangular Cholesky factor K. Mirrors MATLAB hist_decmp(y, beta, residuals, c, p, K, series).

title: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
code: fhistdec(y, fVAR, K, series)

title: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
text: y TxN numeric matrix original (undemeaned) data. fVAR List returned fVAR(), containing minimum beta, residuals, sigma_full, p, c, n_exog. K NxN lower-triangular Cholesky factor residual covariance matrix (.e. t(chol(sigma_full))). series Integer (1-indexed) selecting variable decompose.

title: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
text: list two elements: histdec (T-p) x N numeric matrix. Column j     cumulative contribution structural shock j chosen     variable point time. ystar (T-p) numeric vector demeaned realisation     chosen variable (benchmark series plot).

title: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
text: structural shocks recovered $$\varepsilon_t = K^{-1} u_t$$ \(u_t\) reduced-form residuals. structural MA representation built multiplying Wold matrix K: $$\Theta_h = \Psi_h K$$ contribution shock \(j\) variable \(\) time \(t\) inner product \(\Theta_{0:t}[,j]\) time-reversed structural shocks \(\varepsilon_{t:-1:0,j}\): $$HD(t,j) = \sum_{h=0}^{t} \Theta_h[,j]\, \varepsilon_{t-h,j}$$

title: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ var_result <- fVAR(y, p = 12, c = 1) K <- t(chol(var_result$sigma_full)) series <- match("UNCERT", colnames(y))  hd <- fhistdec(y, var_result, K, series)  # hd$histdec is (T-p) x N; hd$ystar is the demeaned series matplot(hd$histdec, type = "l") lines(hd$ystar, lwd = 2) } # }

title: Create Lagged Matrix — flagmakerMatrix
text: Creates matrix lagged values time series matrix. variable lag 1 p, function creates column containing lagged values, removing first p rows align lags properly.

title: Create Lagged Matrix — flagmakerMatrix
code: flagmakerMatrix(y, p)

title: Create Lagged Matrix — flagmakerMatrix
text: y numeric matrix, data frame, tibble time series data T rows (time periods) N columns (variables) p integer specifying number lags create

title: Create Lagged Matrix — flagmakerMatrix
text: numeric matrix (T-p) rows (N*p) columns. columns   organized : N variables lag 1, N variables lag 2, etc.   Column names generated "var_name_lag_k" applicable.

title: Create Lagged Matrix — flagmakerMatrix
text: function creates lagged matrix column represents   lagged version original variables. lags organized lag   order first, variable. example, 2 variables 2 lags,   column order : var1_lag1, var2_lag1, var1_lag2, var2_lag2.

title: Create Lagged Matrix — flagmakerMatrix
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Create sample data as data frame y <- data.frame(var1 = 1:10, var2 = 11:20)  # Create 2 lags x <- lagmakerMatrix(y, 2)  # Works with tibbles too library(tibble) y_tbl <- tibble(var1 = 1:10, var2 = 11:20) x <- lagmakerMatrix(y_tbl, 2) } # }

what: Moving Block Bootstrap for VAR Residuals and Instruments — fmbb_var
title: Moving Block Bootstrap for VAR Residuals and Instruments — fmbb_var
text: Computes moving-block bootstrap sample reduced form errors VAR(p) model. Optionally bootstraps matrix instruments M.

title: Moving Block Bootstrap for VAR Residuals and Instruments — fmbb_var
code: fmbb_var(eps, lags, BlockSize, M = NULL)

title: Moving Block Bootstrap for VAR Residuals and Instruments — fmbb_var
text: eps (T-p) x N matrix VAR residuals lags Integer lag order p VAR model BlockSize Integer block size moving block bootstrap. 0 negative, automatically computed round(5.03 * T^0.25) M Optional (T-p) x K matrix instruments bootstrap (default = NULL)

title: Moving Block Bootstrap for VAR Residuals and Instruments — fmbb_var
text: list containing: eps_boot: (T-p) x N matrix bootstrapped residuals (centered) M_boot: (T-p) x K matrix bootstrapped instruments (centered),                   empty matrix M provided

title: Moving Block Bootstrap for VAR Residuals and Instruments — fmbb_var
text: moving block bootstrap preserves temporal dependence structure residuals. default block size follows recommendation "Proxy SVARs: Asymptotic Theory, Bootstrap Inference, Effects Income Tax Changes United States". residuals instruments centered preserve sample properties. instruments, non-zero values centered (handle sparse instruments).

title: Moving Block Bootstrap for VAR Residuals and Instruments — fmbb_var
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Bootstrap residuals only eps <- matrix(rnorm(200), 100, 2) result <- fmbb_var(eps, lags = 2, BlockSize = 0) eps_boot <- result$eps_boot  # Bootstrap residuals and instruments M <- matrix(rnorm(100), 100, 1) result <- fmbb_var(eps, lags = 2, BlockSize = 0, M) M_boot <- result$M_boot } # }

title: Plot Historical Decomposition — fplot_histdec
text: Plots historical decomposition multiple variables, showing contribution chosen structural shock, residual (shocks), total explained variation stacked bar chart total line overlay. Mirrors MATLAB Bloom replication figure facets per variable.

title: Plot Historical Decomposition — fplot_histdec
code: fplot_histdec(   histdec_list,   shock,   shockname,   dates = NULL,   p = 0L,   return_data = FALSE,   facet_ncol = 2,   shock_fill = "#407EC9",   resid_fill = "#E87722",   total_color = "#1a1a1a",   total_linewidth = 0.8,   area_alpha = 1 )

title: Plot Historical Decomposition — fplot_histdec
text: histdec_list Named list (T-p) x N matrices, one per variable plot. element $histdec output fhistdec() called variable. Names used facet labels. shock Integer (1-indexed) selecting shock interest. shockname Character string label selected shock (legend entry). dates Optional vector dates (Date, numeric, integer). Can full T-length vector (trimmed internally using p) already- trimmed (T-p)-length vector. Default NULL uses integer index. p Integer lag order VAR. Used dates length T (full sample) trim dates[(p+1):T]. Default 0L. return_data Logical. TRUE returns wide-format tibble (columns: date, variable, shockname, Residual, Total) instead plot. Default FALSE. facet_ncol Number columns facet grid. Default 2. shock_fill Fill color shock contribution area (default: "#407EC9"). resid_fill Fill color residual (shocks) area (default: "#E87722"). total_color Color total line overlay (default: "#1a1a1a"). total_linewidth Line width total line (default: 0.8). area_alpha Transparency stacked areas (default: 1).

title: Plot Historical Decomposition — fplot_histdec
text: ggplot object (return_data = FALSE) tibble   columns date, variable, series, value.

title: Plot Historical Decomposition — fplot_histdec
text: variable time period t plot shows: Shock bar — histdec[t, shock]: contribution     selected structural shock. Residual bar — sum histdec[t, -shock]: combined     contribution shocks. Total line — sum bars: total explained variation.

title: Plot Historical Decomposition — fplot_histdec
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ var_result <- fVAR(y, p = 12, c = 1) K      <- t(chol(var_result$sigma_full)) shock  <- match("UNCERT", colnames(y)) series <- setdiff(seq_len(ncol(y)), shock)  # Call fhistdec for each variable and collect into a named list histdec_list <- setNames(     lapply(series, function(i) fhistdec(y, var_result, K, i)$histdec),     colnames(y)[series] )  fplot_histdec(     histdec_list = histdec_list,     shock        = shock,     shockname    = "Uncertainty",     dates        = dates_vec,     p            = 12 ) + ftheme_tidyMacro() } # }

what: Plot Forecast Error Variance Decomposition — fplot_vardec
title: Plot Forecast Error Variance Decomposition — fplot_vardec
text: Creates area plots showing forecast error variance decomposition (FEVD) multiple variables across horizons.

title: Plot Forecast Error Variance Decomposition — fplot_vardec
code: fplot_vardec(fevd, varnames, shocknames, return_df = FALSE)

title: Plot Forecast Error Variance Decomposition — fplot_vardec
text: fevd numeric array containing forecast error variance shares. Either 3-dimensional array (N x N x horizon) multiple shocks, 2-dimensional matrix (N x horizon) single shock. varnames Character vector variable names. Length equal N (number variables). shocknames Character vector shock names. 3-dimensional fevd, length equal N. 2-dimensional fevd, single name. return_df Logical. TRUE, returns data frame instead plot. Default FALSE.

title: Plot Forecast Error Variance Decomposition — fplot_vardec
text: return_df = FALSE, returns ggplot object showing   FEVD variable separate facets. return_df = TRUE,   returns data frame long format columns: horizon, variable, shock,   value.

title: Plot Forecast Error Variance Decomposition — fplot_vardec
text: function creates stacked area charts showing forecast error variance decomposed across different shocks forecast horizon. variable displayed separate facet panel. 2-dimensional input (single shock), function automatically calculates displays "Shocks" complement ensure decomposition sums 1.

what: Plot Cholesky Impulse Response Functions with Confidence Bands — fplotirf_chol
title: Plot Cholesky Impulse Response Functions with Confidence Bands — fplotirf_chol
text: Creates grid IRF plots showing responses variables single shock, shaded confidence bands using ggplot2. Designed Cholesky-identified VARs.

title: Plot Cholesky Impulse Response Functions with Confidence Bands — fplotirf_chol
code: fplotirf_chol(   point,   upper,   lower,   shock,   varnames,   prc,   return_data = FALSE,   ribbon_fill = "#407EC9",   ribbon_alpha = 0.3,   line_color = "#910048",   zero_line_color = "#707372",   facet_scales = "free",   facet_ncol = NULL )

title: Plot Cholesky Impulse Response Functions with Confidence Bands — fplotirf_chol
text: point Array point estimates (N x N x horizon) upper Array upper confidence bounds (N x N x horizon) lower Array lower confidence bounds (N x N x horizon) shock Integer, shock plot (1-indexed, typically 1 N) varnames Character vector variable names (length N) prc Numeric, confidence level percentage (e.g., 68 95) return_data Logical, TRUE returns tibble instead plot ribbon_fill Color confidence band fill (default: "#407EC9") ribbon_alpha Transparency confidence band (default: 0.3) line_color Color point estimate line (default: "#910048") zero_line_color Color horizontal zero line (default: "#707372") facet_scales Facet scales option: "free", "free_y", "free_x", "fixed" (default: "free") facet_ncol Number columns facet grid (default: NULL, auto-calculated)

title: Plot Cholesky Impulse Response Functions with Confidence Bands — fplotirf_chol
text: ggplot object (return_data = FALSE) tibble (return_data = TRUE)

title: Plot Cholesky Impulse Response Functions with Confidence Bands — fplotirf_chol
text: function extracts impulse responses N variables specific shock 3D arrays (typically output fbootstrapChol). shock parameter selects column (shock) visualize across response variables. plot uses currently active ggplot2 theme. consistent look tidyMacro plots, use ftheme_tidyMacro() set globally set_tidyMacro_theme().

title: Plot Cholesky Impulse Response Functions with Confidence Bands — fplotirf_chol
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Estimate VAR and compute Cholesky IRF var_result <- fVAR(y, p = 2, c = 1) wold <- fwoldIRF(var_result, horizon = 20) S <- t(chol(var_result$sigma_full)) point_irf <- fcholeskyIRF(wold, S)  # Bootstrap confidence bands boot_result <- fbootstrapChol(y, var_result, nboot = 1000,                                horizon = 20, prc = 68, bootscheme = "residual")  # Plot responses to first shock fplotirf_chol(point_irf, boot_result$upper, boot_result$lower,                shock = 1, varnames = c("GDP", "Inflation", "Interest Rate"),               prc = 68)  # Use custom theme fplotirf_chol(point_irf, boot_result$upper, boot_result$lower,                shock = 1, varnames = c("GDP", "Inflation", "Interest Rate"),               prc = 68) +   ftheme_tidyMacro() } # }

what: Plot Impulse Response Functions with Confidence Bands — fplotirf_iv

text: Creates grid IRF plots shaded confidence bands using ggplot2

title: Plot Impulse Response Functions with Confidence Bands — fplotirf_iv
code: fplotirf_iv(   point,   upper,   lower,   varnames,   shockname,   prc,   return_data = FALSE,   ribbon_fill = "#407EC9",   ribbon_alpha = 0.3,   line_color = "#910048",   zero_line_color = "#707372",   facet_scales = "free",   facet_ncol = 2 )

title: Plot Impulse Response Functions with Confidence Bands — fplotirf_iv

title: Plot Impulse Response Functions with Confidence Bands — fplotirf_iv
text: ggplot object (return_data = FALSE) tibble (return_data = TRUE)

what: Remove Small-Sample Bias from VAR Coefficient Estimates — fremove_bias
title: Remove Small-Sample Bias from VAR Coefficient Estimates — fremove_bias
text: Computes bootstrap mean VAR coefficients, subtracts bias point estimates, iteratively shrinks correction towards zero bias-corrected system explosive (eigenvalues companion matrix must strictly less one).

title: Remove Small-Sample Bias from VAR Coefficient Estimates — fremove_bias
code: fremove_bias(beta, c, p, boot_beta)

title: Remove Small-Sample Bias from VAR Coefficient Estimates — fremove_bias
text: beta (Np+c+M) x N matrix OLS VAR coefficient estimates c Integer intercept indicator (1 intercept included, 0 otherwise) p Integer VAR lag order boot_beta (Np+c+M) x N x nboot cube bootstrapped VAR coefficient estimates collected first-pass bootstrap

title: Remove Small-Sample Bias from VAR Coefficient Estimates — fremove_bias
text: list elements:   Beta: N x (Np+c+M) bias-corrected coefficient matrix (transposed         relative input matches convention used         fbootstrapCholCorrected)   corrections: integer count iterative shrinkage steps applied                (1 means first attempt already stable)

title: Remove Small-Sample Bias from VAR Coefficient Estimates — fremove_bias
text: bias estimated bias = mean(boot_beta, dim=3) - beta' original estimates already explosive function returns uncorrected estimates.  Otherwise function applies full bias correction checks stability.  corrected system still explosive (rare practice) reduces correction factor one percent per iteration stability achieved 200 attempts exhausted, point uncorrected estimates returned. mirrors remove_bias.m MATLAB reference implementation.

what: Custom ggplot2 Theme for tidyMacro Package — ftheme_tidyMacro
title: Custom ggplot2 Theme for tidyMacro Package — ftheme_tidyMacro
text: customized ggplot2 theme based theme_bw() Palatino font family, adjusted grid lines, centered plot elements. Designed professional publications presentations VAR model results.

title: Custom ggplot2 Theme for tidyMacro Package — ftheme_tidyMacro
code: ftheme_tidyMacro(base_size = 14)

title: Custom ggplot2 Theme for tidyMacro Package — ftheme_tidyMacro
text: base_size Numeric. Base font size points. Default 14. text sizes scaled relative value. plot title 1.5 times base size.

title: Custom ggplot2 Theme for tidyMacro Package — ftheme_tidyMacro
text: ggplot2 theme object can added ggplot2 plots. theme inherits theme_bw() modifies specific elements create consistent visual style suitable economic time series impulse response function plots.

title: Custom ggplot2 Theme for tidyMacro Package — ftheme_tidyMacro
text: Palatino font family must available system proper rendering. Palatino available, ggplot2 fall back default font family. systems, can check available fonts using extrafont::fonts() loading extrafont package.

title: Custom ggplot2 Theme for tidyMacro Package — ftheme_tidyMacro
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(ggplot2) library(tidyMacro)  # Basic usage with default base size ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +   geom_point() +   labs(title = "Miles per Gallon vs Weight") +   ftheme_tidyMacro()  # Customize base size ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +   geom_point(size = 3) +   labs(title = "MPG vs Weight by Cylinders",        x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",        y = "Miles per Gallon") +   scale_color_tidyMacro() +   ftheme_tidyMacro(base_size = 16) } # }

what: Compute Wold Impulse Response Functions for VAR Model — fwoldIRF
title: Compute Wold Impulse Response Functions for VAR Model — fwoldIRF
text: Compute Wold Impulse Response Functions VAR Model

title: Compute Wold Impulse Response Functions for VAR Model — fwoldIRF
code: fwoldIRF(fVAR, horizon)

title: Compute Wold Impulse Response Functions for VAR Model — fwoldIRF
text: fVAR list containing VAR estimation results elements: beta: Coefficient matrix c: Integer indicator intercept (1 intercept, 0 otherwise) p: Integer lag order n_exog: Number exogenous variables (optional) horizon Integer, IRF horizon (number periods ahead)

title: Compute Wold Impulse Response Functions for VAR Model — fwoldIRF
text: 3D array (cube) Wold impulse response functions dimensions   N x N x (horizon+1), irfwold[,,h] contains response horizon h

title: Compute Wold Impulse Response Functions for VAR Model — fwoldIRF
text: function computes Wold (reduced-form) impulse response functions VAR(p) model optional exogenous variables calculating powers companion matrix. Wold IRF shows effect one-unit shock variable variables system time, without imposing identifying restrictions. Exogenous variables contribute dynamic propagation shocks therefore excluded companion matrix representation. IRFs computed : $$\Phi_h = ^h[1:N, 1:N]$$ companion matrix h horizon.

title: Compute Wold Impulse Response Functions for VAR Model — fwoldIRF
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # VAR(2) model with 3 variables and 1 exogenous variable y <- matrix(rnorm(200), ncol = 2) exog <- matrix(rnorm(100), ncol = 1) VAR <- fVAR(y, p = 2, c = 1, exog = exog) irf <- fwoldIRF(VAR, horizon = 10) # irf is a 2x2x11 array  # Plot impulse response plot(irf[1, 2, ], type = "l",       main = "Response of Variable 2 to Shock in Variable 1") } # }

text: ggplot2 color scale can added plot.

title: tidyMacro Color Scale for ggplot2 — scale_color_tidyMacro
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(ggplot2) library(tidyMacro)  # IRF plot with multiple variables ggplot(irf_data, aes(x = horizon, y = response, color = variable)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +   labs(title = "Impulse Response Functions",        x = "Horizon",        y = "Response") +   scale_color_tidyMacro() +   ftheme_tidyMacro()  # Reverse color order ggplot(fevd_data, aes(x = horizon, y = share, color = shock)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   scale_color_tidyMacro(reverse = TRUE) +   ftheme_tidyMacro() } # }

what: Update ggplot2 Theme Defaults to tidyMacro Style — set_tidyMacro_theme
title: Update ggplot2 Theme Defaults to tidyMacro Style — set_tidyMacro_theme

title: Update ggplot2 Theme Defaults to tidyMacro Style — set_tidyMacro_theme
code: set_tidyMacro_theme(base_size = 14)

title: Update ggplot2 Theme Defaults to tidyMacro Style — set_tidyMacro_theme
text: base_size Numeric. Base font size theme. Default 14.

title: Update ggplot2 Theme Defaults to tidyMacro Style — set_tidyMacro_theme
text: Invisibly returns NULL. Called side effects.

title: Update ggplot2 Theme Defaults to tidyMacro Style — set_tidyMacro_theme
text: function modifies default ggplot2 theme discrete color/fill scales current session. plots created calling function automatically use tidyMacro styling unless explicitly overridden. revert ggplot2 defaults, use ggplot2::theme_set(ggplot2::theme_gray()) remove scale defaults ggplot2::update_geom_defaults().

title: Update ggplot2 Theme Defaults to tidyMacro Style — set_tidyMacro_theme
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(ggplot2) library(tidyMacro)  # Set tidyMacro as default theme for the session set_tidyMacro_theme()  # All subsequent plots will use tidyMacro styling ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +   geom_point()  # Revert to ggplot2 defaults theme_set(theme_gray()) } # }

what: tidyMacro: Fast Vector Autoregression Models via C++ — tidyMacro-package
title: tidyMacro: Fast Vector Autoregression Models via C++ — tidyMacro-package
text: Implements Vector Autoregression (VAR) models using fast efficient C++ programs via Rcpp RcppArmadillo. Provides tools estimation, forecasting, impulse response analysis.

title: tidyMacro: Fast Vector Autoregression Models via C++ — tidyMacro-package
text: Maintainer: Muhsin Ciftci farmer.muhsin@gmail.com

title: fastVARs Color Palette — tidyMacro_colors
text: Official color palette tidyMacro package, designed VAR model visualizations including impulse response functions, forecast error variance decompositions, time series plots.

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Display the color palette scales::show_col(tidyMacro_colors)  # Use with ggplot2 library(ggplot2) ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +   geom_point() +   scale_color_manual(values = tidyMacro_colors) +   ftheme_tidyMacro() } # }
